verses.text: If there be a controversy, they come unto judgment, the judges shall judge, justify the righteous, condemn the wicked. If the wicked be worthy to be beaten, the judge shall cause him to lie down, be beaten according to his fault. Forty stripes he may give him; not exceed — lest if he should exceed, thy brother seem vile unto thee. Guidance: A maximum on punishment. Forty was the ceiling — even the criminal stayed your brother. The verse names what corporal punishment can do to the victim: making him 'seem vile' in his community. God built dignity into the discipline. (Synagogue tradition stopped at thirty-nine to be safe.)

Thou shalt not muzzle the ox when he treadeth out the corn. Guidance: A working animal eats from his work. The ox grinding the grain takes a mouthful as he walks. Don't begrudge him. Paul will quote this twice (1 Cor 9, 1 Tim 5) to argue that those who labor in the gospel deserve to eat from it. The verse about an ox turns out to be about people.

If brethren dwell together, and one die without child, the wife shall not marry a stranger: her husband's brother shall take her to wife. The firstborn she beareth shall succeed in the dead brother's name. If he refuse, his brother's wife shall go to the elders, loose his shoe, spit in his face: So shall it be done unto him that will not build up his brother's house. Guidance: Levirate marriage — the brother-in-law protected a widow from social erasure and the dead man's name from extinction. If he refused, the widow's public shoe-and-spit ritual marked him for life. Boaz would later honor this principle; in Ruth 4 he removes a shoe before the elders.

When men strive together, and the wife of the one draweth near to deliver her husband out of the hand of him that smiteth him, putteth forth her hand, taketh him by the secrets: then thou shalt cut off her hand; thine eye shall not pity her. Guidance: A startling penalty — and not one to romanticize. The text addresses a specific assault that could maim a man's reproductive line. The law treats it as a serious bodily crime requiring serious bodily penalty. Like other extreme penalties, it stood as a deterrent more than a practice. Most Jewish tradition converted physical cutoff to a fine.

Thou shalt not have in thy bag divers weights, a great and a small. Thou shalt not have in thine house divers measures, a great and a small. Thou shalt have a perfect and just weight, a perfect and just measure: that thy days may be lengthened in the land. For all that do such things, all that do unrighteously, are an abomination unto the LORD thy God. Guidance: Two scales is the merchant's oldest cheat — heavy when he buys, light when he sells. God names dual measures 'abomination' and links honesty in the market to length of days in the land. Crooked scales shorten nations.

Remember what Amalek did unto thee by the way, when ye were come forth out of Egypt; how he met thee by the way, smote the hindmost of thee, even all that were feeble behind thee, when thou wast faint and weary; he feared not God. Therefore, when the LORD thy God hath given thee rest, thou shalt blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven; thou shalt not forget it. Guidance: God remembers what the weak suffer. Amalek struck the stragglers — the elderly, the children, the exhausted. That cowardice could not be forgotten. The God of the worn-out keeps the file open until justice catches up. (Saul will be commanded to execute this in 1 Samuel 15.)
